title: Implement ARM PMULL carry-less multiplication kernels for GF(2^m)
description:
Implement carry-less multiplication kernels for GF(2^m) using ARM NEON `PMULL`/`PMULL2` instructions. This is the AArch64 counterpart to the x86 `PCLMULQDQ` kernels (055bda14).

## Target instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Width |
|-------------|-----------|-------|
| `PMULL` | Polynomial multiply (lower 64 bits) | 64×64→128 bit |
| `PMULL2` | Polynomial multiply (upper 64 bits) | 64×64→128 bit |

These are available on all ARMv8-A (AArch64) processors with the Crypto extensions, which includes virtually all modern ARM chips (Apple Silicon, Cortex-A53+, Graviton).

## Kernel API

```rust
// In gf2-kernels-simd
/// Carry-less multiplication of two 64-bit polynomials using ARM PMULL.
pub unsafe fn clmul_u64_neon(a: u64, b: u64) -> u128;

/// Carry-less multiplication + Barrett reduction for GF(2^m).
pub unsafe fn clmul_reduce_u64_neon(a: u64, b: u64, reducer: &BarrettReducer) -> u64;

/// Batch carry-less multiplication using PMULL/PMULL2 interleaving.
pub unsafe fn clmul_batch_neon(a: &[u64], b: &[u64], out: &mut [u128]);
```

## Implementation notes

- Use `vmull_p64` / `vmull_high_p64` intrinsics from `core::arch::aarch64`.
- The PMULL instruction has the same semantics as x86 `PCLMULQDQ` (carry-less multiply of two 64-bit values producing a 128-bit result), so the Barrett reduction logic from 0458fa04 applies identically.
- For batch operations, interleave `PMULL` and `PMULL2` to process two multiplications per NEON register pair.
- Runtime detection via `std::arch::is_aarch64_feature_detected!("pmull")`.

## Location

`crates/gf2-kernels-simd/src/arm/clmul.rs` (new file, new `arm/` module)

## Dependencies

None — this is a leaf task. Barrett reduction (0458fa04) is shared but developed independently.

## Success criteria

- Produces identical results to scalar Karatsuba for all m=2..16.
- Throughput improvement: ≥3× over scalar for single GF(2^8) multiplication on Apple M-series or Graviton.
- CI cross-compilation passes (may need `cross` or QEMU for testing on x86 CI runners).
